_comment: English → Hindi | Month 1 Week 2 | Lesson — Numbers & Colors
title: Numbers, Colors, and Daily Objects
description: Learn to count to 10 in Hindi, name basic colors, and identify everyday items.

blocks.title: Counting in Hindi
blocks.body:
Numbers (संख्या - Sankhya) in Hindi are very important for everyday life!

1: एक (Ek)
2: दो (Do)
3: तीन (Teen)
4: चार (Chaar)
5: पाँच (Paanch)
6: छह (Chhah)
7: सात (Saat)
8: आठ (Aath)
9: नौ (Nau)
10: दस (Das)

Counting from 1 to 10 will help you shop, ask for the time, and talk about quantities.

blocks.title: This Week's Focus
points: Asking 'How much is it?' (Kitne ka hai?)

blocks.title: Yah and Vah (यह और वह)
blocks.pattern: Yah [Object] hai (This is...) / Vah [Object] hai (That is...)
blocks.note: 'Ek' means 'one' but is used like 'a/an' in English. Yah is used for objects near you, and Vah is used for objects far from you.

lines.text: नमस्ते! यह कितना है? (Namaste! Yah kitna hai?)
यह दस रुपये है। (Yah das rupaye hai.)
मुझे यह लाल वाला चाहिए। (Mujhe yah laal vala chahiye.)

blocks.body: 💡 Pro tip: In Hindi, adjectives often change their ending depending on the gender of the noun. 'Laal' (red) doesn't change, but 'Neela' (blue - masculine) becomes 'Neeli' (blue - feminine)!

blocks.title: Number Check
blocks.instructions: Fill in the correct Hindi number (Romaji):

items.sentence: The color of the sky is ___ (blue).

blocks.title: Bargaining in India
blocks.content: Bargaining (Bhav-tav) is very common in Indian street markets! Knowing your numbers in Hindi goes a long way. If something is too expensive, you can say 'Bohot mehenga hai' (It is very expensive) and offer a lower price. Shopkeepers often love it when foreigners speak a little Hindi!

blocks.title: Week 2 Checkpoint
items: Ask for prices using 'Yah kitna hai?'
